candidates.direction_name: Restore The Accepted Compact PTX Anchor After The Failed Existing X32 Probe
ranking_notes: This is the highest-value move because the regression is large, causal, and localized to the hot-band dispatch choice.
The accepted compact anchor is already measured on this branch, so recovery is more valuable than searching inside the broken x32 branch.
Frontier should prefer this over older restore candidates because it is tied to the latest live regression and carries the strongest current evidence.
candidates.hypothesis: The current hot-band dispatch is sitting on a clearly regressed x32 staged branch. Restoring the accepted compact PTX microkernel should immediately recover occupancy, shared-memory footprint, and the known-good branch surface before the search spends another round on new ideas.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: The immediate bottleneck is not an unresolved compact-surface seam; it is the residency and sync damage introduced by the x32 staged probe.
candidates.risk: Low. This is a bounded recovery back to the best-known refactor-path anchor rather than a fresh algorithmic change.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if restoring the compact anchor requires changes beyond the fixed hot-band dispatch and its directly paired kernel selection.
expected_local_changes: Switch the fixed hot-band dispatch back from the existing 128x128x32 staged kernel to the compact 128x128 PTX microkernel.
Leave grouped_rows=4, the peeled hot-band row band, and the 64x96 tail path unchanged.
Do not bundle the restore with another sync or tiling experiment in the same round.

candidates.direction_name: Reopen Compact Barrier Trims Only After The Anchor Is Back
ranking_notes: This family still points at the right hotspot neighborhood, but the restore has higher value and lower ambiguity than another seam tweak on the broken branch.
The recent small-regression history means this should only be revisited once the anchor is back and remeasured.
Keeping it second preserves a low-footprint next step without reopening the failed large-footprint families.
candidates.hypothesis: Once the accepted compact anchor is restored, the remaining optimization room is still most plausibly in small CTA-sync trims that avoid increasing the shared-memory or register footprint. Those ideas should stay behind the restore, not ahead of it.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Residual barrier overhead on the compact PTX surface after the broken x32 branch is removed.
candidates.risk: Moderate. The family targets the right hotspot class, but the branch already has evidence that tiny seam edits can flatten out quickly.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the proposed trim would replay the same refill-before-wait or final-no-refill-sync skip variants already measured in rounds 13-15.
expected_local_changes: Keep any follow-up confined to the compact PTX loop sync cadence.
Do not change CTA geometry, grouped-row mapping, or tail launches when revisiting this family.

candidates.direction_name: Only Revisit X32 Staging If Its Register And Shared-Memory Footprint Is Cut Materially
ranking_notes: This stays in the set only to keep the search ledger honest about why the staged family is being deprioritized.
The family should not be selected again without a concrete plan to lower both registers per thread and shared memory materially versus the live failed probe.
It ranks last because the current branch already measured a decisive negative outcome for the existing x32 kernel.
candidates.hypothesis: The x32 idea is not categorically impossible, but the existing implementation is too expensive in residency terms. The only reason to reopen this family later would be a materially smaller register/shared-memory footprint, not another like-for-like probe.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Occupancy and latency hiding would remain the dominant failure mode unless the staged kernel's resident footprint is reduced substantially.
candidates.risk: High. The live evidence says the current staged branch is a large regression, so this family should stay dormant unless its footprint changes materially.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the proposed x32 follow-up keeps the same launch footprint class or merely shuffles waits/barriers without reducing registers or shared memory.
expected_local_changes: Keep the family closed until an x32 variant explicitly reduces its resident footprint relative to the current failed implementation.

notes: Projected exactly three node_b directions into normalized search candidates.
